actions.thought: On the Button in a 3-handed game, I have a wide opening range, but 93s is at the bottom end of that range and typically considered a fold in a professional setting. Raising would be the only alternative to folding, as limping is suboptimal, but with such a weak hand early in the tournament, I prefer to fold and wait for better opportunities.

I am in the small blind with 9-10 offsuit, heads-up against the big blind after the button folded. With a hand that plays well enough in position post-flop but isn't premium, raising to 250 provides initiative and pressure, allowing me to potentially take down the blinds immediately or control the hand if the big blind calls.

I am in the Big Blind with 3s 5h, an extremely weak, unsuited, and disconnected hand. The Small Blind has raised to 250 (2.5x the big blind). I have very little equity against their opening range, and playing a bloated pot out of position or in position with such marginal cards is a losing play. Folding is the straightforward and correct decision.

I am on the button with Ace-Five offsuit, which is a strong hand to open in late position. By raising to 250 (2.5x the big blind), I can exert pressure on the blinds and potentially win the pot pre-flop or take control with position on the flop.

8s9s is a great suited connector that plays well post-flop. Facing a Button open, 3-betting from the Small Blind helps isolate the raiser, takes the initiative, and forces the Big Blind out, while avoiding playing a multi-way pot out of position.

I am holding 8h 3s in the Big Blind. The Button opened to 250, and the Small Blind 3-bet to 850. 83o is a very weak hand with zero playability against a raise and a re-raise. I have no incentive to call or bluff in this spot, especially being out of position. Folding is the standard and correct play.

I opened on the button with Ace-Five offsuit, and the small blind 3-bet to 850. While Ace-Five offsuit is a marginal hand, being in position provides significant value. The pot odds justify a call (600 to win 1200), and I prefer to see a flop to evaluate my relative strength before committing more chips. Folding here would be overly tight, and a 4-bet is unnecessary risk this early in the tournament.

I completely missed the flop with 8s9s on a Qd 6d 4c board. Out of position against a 3-bet calling range, c-betting here with zero equity and no meaningful draws is too risky. Checking with the intention to fold to a bet is the most prudent line, saving chips for better spots.

The flop of 4c 6d Qd provides me with the nut flush draw and an overcard. Given that my opponent checked to me in this 3-bet pot, betting is an excellent way to realize my equity. It allows me to potentially take down the pot immediately or build a larger pot if I connect with my diamond draw or Ace. A bet of 700 chips into the 1800 pot is a standard sizing that pressures my opponent's range while keeping the pot manageable.

As planned, I completely missed this flop with no pair, no meaningful draw, and no backdoor flush draw since I hold spades and the board has diamonds and clubs. Facing a bet of 700, continuing with 9-high out of position is throwing good chips after bad. Folding is the only correct play here.
Q3o is a weak offsuit hand. Even from the Button, it is unprofitable to play against the blinds. Folding is the standard play here.

I am in the Small Blind with Jc 7d, a marginal offsuit hand. The Button (Pro) has folded, leaving only the Big Blind (Flashlight) to act. While this is a steal opportunity, Flashlight has shown significant aggression in the first two hands. J7o plays poorly out of position and is generally below the threshold for a profitable open-raise or call in this spot. Folding preserves my stack for better spots.

I am on the button with 8c 2c. While suited, this hand is extremely weak and does not play well post-flop. In a three-handed game, the button range should be wide, but 8-high with a deuce kicker is near the bottom of any reasonable opening range. Given the early stage of the tournament, I will fold and wait for a better opportunity rather than risking chips with a marginal hand.

With the button folding, I am heads-up against the big blind. Holding Ace-Queen offsuit, I have a significant range advantage. Raising to 350 is a strong move to take initiative, potentially take down the pot immediately, or set myself up for a favorable continuation bet on most flops. This size is standard to pressure the big blind without over-committing.

Facing a 3.5x open from the Small Blind, J9 offsuit is too weak to defend profitably. Even though I would have position post-flop, the hand lacks the equity and playability needed to call a large raise or 3-bet as a bluff. Folding is the standard and most mathematically sound decision here.

I hold Ace-King offsuit, a premium starting hand. As the button, I have position and the initiative. Raising is the standard play to build value and potentially isolate the blinds. I will open to 300, which is consistent with previous sizing and a standard open for 3BB.

I am in the Small Blind with 5s 6c, an offsuit connector. Facing a 3x raise from the Button, this hand is too weak to call or 3-bet, especially being out of position post-flop. The lack of suitedness drastically reduces its playability and equity. Folding is the only sensible choice.

QJ is a strong hand to defend the Big Blind with against a standard 3x open from the Button. While raising is an option, calling allows me to see a flop and realize equity without bloating the pot while out of position. The pot odds (200 to call into a total pot of 450) make this a very profitable call.

On this Ace-high board, the pre-flop raiser (Flashlight) has a significant range advantage. My hand, Queen-Jack offsuit, has completely missed the flop and possesses very little equity. Leading out (donk betting) into the aggressor on an Ace-high board is rarely optimal. Therefore, I will check to the raiser and most likely fold to any significant continuation bet.

I have top pair, top kicker (Ace-King) on a relatively dry board of 3-5-A. This is a very strong holding. Flash checked to me, and as the pre-flop aggressor, I should continue betting for value. A bet of 250 represents approximately 40% of the pot, which is a solid sizing to extract value from weaker Ace-high hands or pocket pairs, while maintaining control.

On this Ace-high board (3s 5c Ad), the pre-flop raiser (Flashlight) has a significant range advantage. My hand, Queen-Jack offsuit, has completely missed and offers no real drawing potential other than very unlikely runner-runner scenarios. Despite the small bet size from Flashlight, calling out of position with air is a losing play in the long run. I will fold and preserve my stack.
JTo is a standard open-raise from the button in a 3-handed game. It has decent playability and I want to take the initiative to steal the blinds or play in position post-flop.

I am in the Small Blind with 6s 3d, which is an extremely weak hand. The Button (Pro) has opened with a raise to 250. Calling out of position with such a poor hand is a losing play in the long run, and there is still the Big Blind (Flashlight) to act behind me. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am in the big blind with Q6 offsuit, facing a 2.5x raise from the button. The pot odds are favorable (150 to win 400), making a call a standard defensive play to defend my blind. I will see the flop and re-evaluate.

I am in the big blind out of position against the pre-flop raiser. The board of 9-2-4 is dry and my Queen-high hand has no pair or draw. Checking is the standard play to control the pot size and see if the opponent decides to continue or check back.

On a dry, disconnected flop like 9c 2h 4d, I have a significant range advantage as the pre-flop raiser. A continuation bet of around 1/3 to 1/2 pot is standard to fold out my opponent's unpaired overcards and take down the pot right here. With JTo, I have two overcards and some backdoor straight equity, making this a good candidate for a c-bet.

The board is dry, and my Queen-high hand has no pair or draw, meaning I have effectively no equity. Pro's bet of 200 into a 750 pot is a small sizing, but with no way to improve to a winning hand against his likely range, calling out of position is unprofitable. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve my stack.

Ten-Seven offsuit is a marginal hand to open from the Button in a three-handed game. Given that the blinds (Flashlight and Pro) have shown significant aggression in previous hands, I will fold this hand to avoid getting into a difficult post-flop situation or facing a 3-bet with a weak holding.

With 8-5 suited in the small blind against the big blind, I have a decent equity edge to play the hand aggressively. Raising to 250 builds the pot and allows me to dictate the betting, testing the big blind's hand while gaining the initiative.

With 6h 2d offsuit, my hand is extremely weak and virtually unplayable in this situation. Facing a raise from the small blind, the pot odds do not justify calling with such a poor holding. The optimal play is simply to fold and wait for a better spot to invest chips.

Ten-four offsuit is an extremely weak starting hand with very poor playability and showdown value. Even though I am on the button, this hand does not justify an opening raise or a call of the big blind. Folding is the most disciplined and profitable play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Flashlight folded on the button, leaving me blind versus blind against Flash. Q9 offsuit is a strong enough hand to open-raise from the small blind. Given Flash has been playing relatively tight in previous hands, I will raise to 300 to put pressure on the big blind and try to take down the dead money pre-flop, while having decent equity if called.

I am in the Big Blind with Q3o. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. While I am getting 2:1 odds on a call, Q3o is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position post-flop. In a blind-vs-blind battle, Q3o is generally outside of a standard defending range against a 3x open, and Pro has shown aggression in previous hands. Folding is the most disciplined play to conserve chips for better spots.

Ten-deuce offsuit is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Despite being on the Button, there is no strategic justification for entering the pot with this hand, as it has almost zero playability or equity against any calling range.

In the Small Blind with King-Deuce offsuit (K2o) after the Button has folded, I'm faced with a decision against an aggressive Big Blind (Flashlight). While a King-high hand has some showdown value, K2o is one of the weakest King-X hands and performs poorly out of position (OOP). Given that Flashlight has shown significant aggression in previous hands (raising frequently), I expect a high probability of being re-raised or facing difficult post-flop decisions. At this early stage of the tournament (Hand 9 of 150) and with a healthy stack, I prefer to fold this marginal hand and wait for a better spot rather than playing a bloated pot OOP with a weak kicker.

I am on the button with Jack-Deuce offsuit (J2o). This is one of the weakest hands in Texas Hold'em and generally does not play well post-flop, even with positional advantage. In a three-handed game, I should be playing wider than in a full-ring game, but J2o is still well below the threshold for a standard opening raise. Given the aggressive tendencies shown by Flashlight and Pro in previous hands, attempting a steal here is low-percentage. I will fold and wait for a better starting hand.

With Ace-Trey suited (Ac, 3c) in the small blind and the button having folded, I have a strong enough hand for a 3-handed dynamic to open the pot. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard aggressive play to take control of the pot and potentially force the big blind to fold, or play a post-flop hand with the initiative.

I'm in the big blind facing a raise from the small blind. My hand is Ten-Five offsuit, which is a very weak and disconnected hand. Despite getting some pot odds to call, playing T5o is fundamentally unprofitable against a raise. Folding is the best choice here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am holding 7h 3c, which is a very weak hand with no strategic value. In a three-handed game, opening with such a bottom-tier hand from the button is incorrect. Conserving chips early in the tournament is more important than contesting marginal pots with trash hands. Therefore, I will fold.

With A5o in the Small Blind and the Button folding, I have a good opportunity to attack the Big Blind. A5o is ahead of most random BB hands and has good blocker value. I'll raise to 300 to try and win the pot uncontested or gain the initiative going to the flop.

I am in the big blind with 9d 3h. The small blind (Pro) has raised to 300. Although I am getting 2:1 odds to call, 93o is an extremely weak hand that performs poorly post-flop, even against a wide small blind opening range. Pro has shown aggression from the small blind previously. I will fold here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Pocket eights is a strong starting hand in 3-handed play. As the first to act on the Button, I want to open with a standard raise to take the initiative, build the pot, and potentially isolate an opponent or win the blinds outright.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Six offsuit. The Button (Pro) has opened with a standard raise to 250. Ten-Six offsuit is a very weak hand to play out of position against a button open, especially with the Big Blind still to act. Folding is the most prudent play to conserve my stack for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind with K3s, which is a reasonable hand to defend against a Button raise. Given the pot odds, calling the 150 chips to see a flop is a standard play. My hand has decent post-flop potential if I hit a flush or pair, and folding would be too exploitable against a frequent opener.

I am in the Big Blind with King-high and no meaningful connection to the 2h-6d-Qd board. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, the standard play is to check to the button and evaluate their reaction. With no pair and only backdoor flush possibilities, I should play cautiously and check-fold to any significant betting pressure.

On a 2h-6d-Qd flop, pocket eights give me a solid second pair. The board has a flush draw, and I hold the 8 of diamonds, offering some backdoor equity. A small continuation bet can extract value from worse pairs (like a 6 or pocket 7s) and draws, while protecting my hand from two overcards peeling off on the turn. Checking might invite bluffs from overcards, so betting takes the initiative.

I am in the Big Blind with King-high and no meaningful connection to the 2h-6d-Qd board. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor and have no pair or significant draw, calling a continuation bet is not mathematically or strategically sound. Folding is the correct disciplined play here to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with a premium starting hand (King-Queen suited). In a 3-handed game, this is an extremely strong hand that should be played aggressively for value and to take control of the pot. I will raise to 3x the big blind to build the pot while holding position for the remainder of the hand.

I hold King-Ten offsuit in the small blind, which is a playable hand against a button open. While playing out of position can be challenging, folding would be too tight given the stack depth and the opponent's aggressive opening range. Calling allows me to realize my equity in a raised pot, keeping the price reasonable while navigating post-flop play.

4s 8d is a very weak, unsuited hand. Even though I am getting okay pot odds to complete the bet from the big blind, playing this trash hand out of position against a pre-flop raiser and a caller is negative expected value. Folding is the easiest and most profitable choice.

I have top pair with a decent kicker on a relatively dry board. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop raiser, checking is the optimal strategy. This allows me to gauge the opponent's strength and control the pot size, while also potentially inducing a continuation bet from a bluff or weaker hand.

I have flopped top pair with a strong Queen kicker on a relatively dry board of 4-5-K. As the pre-flop aggressor, I have a range advantage and the best hand most of the time here. Flashlight's check indicates weakness or a marginal hand like a pocket pair or a weaker King. I will place a continuation bet of 400 into the 700 pot to extract value from those weaker hands and to protect against potential draws or an Ace on the turn.

The opponent's continuation bet of 400 into a 700 pot on a King-high board is standard, and my top pair with a Ten kicker is a solid holding that likely dominates a large portion of the opponent's range, including bluffs and weaker pairs. Calling is the most balanced approach here; it keeps the opponent's bluffing range in the hand while avoiding over-committing to a pot if I am against a better king or a set. Raising would likely fold out the hands I want to keep in and isolate me against better hands, so I will proceed with a call to see the turn.

The turn nine of clubs is a relatively safe card that does not significantly improve many of the opponent's likely holdings. I hold top pair with a ten kicker, which is a strong value hand, but since I am out of position, I need to be cautious. Checking allows me to control the pot size and act based on the opponent's response. Betting here could alienate weaker hands I want to keep in, or isolate myself against a better king or a set. Check-calling or check-folding remains the most balanced approach.

I have top pair with a strong kicker (KQ) on a board that hasn't changed much with the 9c. Flashlight's check-call on the flop and check on the turn suggest they are likely holding a weaker King or a middle pair. I will bet 800 into the 1500 pot to extract value from these hands and maintain my lead in the pot.

With top pair and a solid kicker on this turn, I am ahead of the opponent's bluffing range and potential weaker made hands like lower pairs or a weak king. While the nine of clubs adds a flush draw to the board, it doesn't immediately change the strength of my hand. The opponent's turn bet of 800 is standard and offers reasonable pot odds to continue. Calling is the most disciplined play; it allows me to keep the opponent's weaker holdings in the hand while avoiding bloating the pot in an out-of-position scenario where I could be against a set or better two-pair combinations.

On the river, I hold a pair of Kings with a Queen kicker. The board shows no obvious flush or straight completion, but it is a relatively coordinated board. Given the opponent's aggressive betting pattern on the flop and turn, they could be betting with a variety of hands, ranging from worse pairs and bluffs to better hands like sets or two-pair combinations. Betting myself would likely isolate me against stronger hands that have me beat while folding out the bluffs I want to keep in the pot. Checking allows me to control the pot size and act as a bluff-catcher, which is the most disciplined and balanced approach given my position out of the dealer button.

I have improved to top two pair (Kings and Queens) on the river. Flashlight's passive line of check-calling on the flop and turn suggests a relatively strong but non-nutted hand, most likely a King with a medium kicker. While there is a small possibility of a straight with Jack-Ten or a slow-played set, my two pair is extremely strong in a 3-handed game. I will place a value bet of 1600 into the 3100 pot, which is roughly half-pot, to entice a call from a King or even a stubborn Queen.

I hold top pair with a King and a Ten kicker. The river Queen of Spades is a somewhat concerning card as it could have improved the opponent's range (e.g., A-Q), but it is also a plausible card for a triple-barrel bluff. Given the betting sequence, the opponent could definitely be value-betting a weaker King (like K-Jack or K-Nine) or a weaker pair. The pot odds offer me a good price to call (approximately 25% equity required). Folding would be too conservative against a potentially polarized range, so I will call to showdown.

In a three-handed game, holding Q9o on the button allows for a wide opening range to apply pressure on the blinds. Although the hand is marginal, the positional advantage is significant, and I aim to steal the blinds or take initiative in the hand. A raise to 250 is a standard sizing to accomplish this.
I am in the small blind with an extremely weak offsuit hand (2d 8c). Facing a raise from the button, the only viable and profitable option is to fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Kc 5s and facing a button raise to 250 from Flashlight. The Small Blind has folded. I need to call 150 more to see a flop in a pot that will be 550 total. This gives me excellent pot odds (around 3.6:1). While K5o is a relatively weak hand and I will be out of position, it is standard to defend the Big Blind against a button open with most King-high hands at this price. Flashlight has been active, and I have a comfortable chip lead. I will call and evaluate the flop.

On a monotone flop of 2c 6c 7c, I have flopped the King-high flush draw with the Kc in my hand. Being out of position against the pre-flop raiser, I will start by checking. This allows me to see Flashlight's reaction to this coordinated board. If they bet, I have a very strong draw and sufficient equity to continue, and if they check back, I get a free card to try and realize my equity or potentially bluff on a later street if the board remains scary for their range.

The board is monotone with three clubs, which is a dangerous texture. Holding the nine of clubs provides a marginal backdoor potential, but I do not have a strong made hand or a significant draw. Since my opponent checked, checking behind is the prudent play to pot control, avoid potential check-raises, and potentially take a free card to see if I can improve on the turn.

The turn brings the Ace of Spades. While this card generally favors the pre-flop raiser's range, Flashlight's check on the monotone flop suggests they may not have a made flush or a very strong hand. I currently hold the King of Clubs, giving me the nut flush draw (assuming the Ace of Clubs is not in play). By checking again, I am playing my draw passively and giving Flashlight the opportunity to bluff or bet a marginal hand like a pair. If Flashlight bets, I have plenty of equity to call and potentially realize my hand on the river. If they check back, I get a free card to hit my flush.

The board has three clubs, making it a very dangerous texture. An Ace fell on the turn, which is a card that hits most ranges, but since my opponent checked, they are either weak or trapping. Given that I have only Queen-high and one club, I do not have enough equity to justify a bet as a bluff into a range that might include an Ace or a flush. Checking behind is the most disciplined play to control the pot size and see a river card for free, preserving my stack for better opportunities.

On the river, the board is 2c-6c-7c-As-Qs. I missed my flush draw and am left with King-high. Flashlight, the pre-flop aggressor, has checked back on both the flop and the turn, which suggests a weak hand or a marginal made hand that is playing cautiously. While I could attempt a bluff to represent a flush or the Ace, the pot is relatively small (550), and my King-high has almost no showdown value. However, checking is the most prudent play here to avoid unnecessary risk; if Flashlight checks back, I get to see their hand for free, and if they bet, I can easily fold as my hand hasn't improved.

I have reached the river with a pair of Queens (Qh paired with the Qs on the board). The betting sequence has been passive, with checks on the flop and turn. Given that the board has a flush possibility (three clubs) and an Ace, checking behind is the best play. Betting would likely only be called by an Ace, a flush, or a better kicker, while I have sufficient showdown value against a range that checked twice. I will take the showdown.

We are first to act on the Button (UTG in 3-handed play) with 8c 6c. Suited connectors and one-gappers are great hands to open with in position. A standard 2.5x open to 250 puts pressure on the blinds to defend and gives us the initiative going into the flop, where we can often win the pot with a continuation bet if we miss or play a big pot if we hit.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Six of hearts. The Button (Pro) has opened for 2.5x the big blind. While the hand is suited, Ten-Six is a relatively weak holding to defend out of position against a player on the button. Calling here would put me in a difficult spot post-flop, playing a bloated pot without initiative and from the worst position. Given my healthy stack and the early stage of the match, I prefer to wait for more favorable opportunities.

K3 offsuit is a very weak hand, especially when playing out of position against a button raise. While the pot odds are tempting, the risk of getting into a difficult post-flop situation with a dominated king is high. It is a standard fold in this spot to preserve my stack.
T8o is a standard opening hand from the button in a 3-handed game. With the chip lead and being in position, I'll put pressure on the blinds with a standard 2.5x raise to 250.

I am in the small blind with 8-6 offsuit, which is a weak hand to play out of position against a button raise. The pot odds do not justify calling with such a marginal holding, and playing post-flop out of position against the button is generally unprofitable. Therefore, I will fold to preserve my stack.

I am in the Big Blind with an offsuit one-gapper (8c 6d). The Button has raised to 2.5x the big blind, and I am out of position. This hand is far too weak to defend pre-flop, so folding is the optimal and standard play.

Holding Eight of Hearts and Jack of Hearts on the button is a strong position to play. While the hand is not premium, it has good playability and suitedness, making it a perfect candidate for an open-raise to steal the blinds and take initiative in the pot. Raising is superior to folding or limping in this position to maximize the chances of winning the blinds pre-flop or continuing with position post-flop.

J7 suited is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position. Facing a 2.5x open from the Button, calling from the Small Blind is unprofitable due to positional disadvantage and the risk of the Big Blind squeezing. 3-betting as a bluff is also suboptimal with this specific hand class compared to better suited connectors or blockers. Folding is the standard and safest option here.

I have a pocket pair of eights, which is a very strong hand in a three-handed game. Flashlight has been opening frequently from the button. I'll put in a three-bet to 750 to take control of the hand and extract value from a potentially wider button range.

Facing a 3-bet from the Big Blind after my open-raise, my Jh 8h hand is too weak to continue. A 3-bet in a 3-handed game generally indicates a strong, linear range, and calling here would require a significant investment in a pot where I am likely behind. Folding is the disciplined choice to maintain stack integrity and avoid playing a bloated pot with a mediocre holding.
T4s is a weak starting hand, well outside standard opening ranges from the Button. Folding is the optimal decision to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

53 offsuit is a very weak hand with poor post-flop playability. Although it only costs 50 more to call from the small blind after the button folds, I will be out of position against Flashlight, who has shown significant aggression in previous hands. Folding is the most disciplined move here to avoid being exploited out of position with a marginal holding.

I am on the button with Ace-Jack offsuit (Ad Jh), which is a very strong hand in a three-handed game. I have the chip lead and should play aggressively from position. A standard 2.5x open is effective here to put pressure on the blinds while maintaining control over the pot size.

Holding 9d3d, this is a very weak hand to defend from the small blind. Being out of position against the button opener makes it difficult to play post-flop, and the risk of being squeezed by the big blind remains. Given the stack depths and the marginal nature of my hand, folding is the most disciplined and profitable play.

Q7 suited is a marginal hand, especially out of position against a Button raise. Without antes in the pot, the pot odds are not compelling enough to defend this wide, and we risk being dominated by better Queens or stronger hands in the opponent's range. Folding is the disciplined choice.

Being on the button with 7h-Th suited gives me a decent position to open the pot. This hand plays well post-flop, and an open raise is a standard move to try and steal the blinds or build a pot in a favorable position. My stack size of 8100 allows for a standard open to 250 chips (2.5x the big blind).

K8o is a weak offsuit hand. Facing a raise from the Button, playing this out of position from the Small Blind will lead to difficult post-flop spots with reverse implied odds. Folding is the standard, safest play here.

I am in the Big Blind with 4h 3s. Flashlight has raised to 250 from the Button. While the pot odds are somewhat tempting (calling 150 to win a total pot of 550), 43o is an extremely weak hand that plays very poorly out of position. Given that I am the chip leader, there is no reason to defend such a weak range against a 2.5x open. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button 3-handed with a suited connector (4s5s). This is a strong enough hand to open with, as it has good post-flop playability and we can often pick up the blinds directly. I will make a standard 2.5x raise to 250.

I am in the Small Blind with QJo. The Button (Pro) has opened for 250. In a 3-handed game, QJo is a strong hand against a wide Button opening range. While calling is an option, 3-betting allows me to take the initiative, potentially win the pot pre-flop, and discourages the Big Blind from squeezing. Given my chip lead and previous aggressive play (Hands 13, 17, 19), a 3-bet to 850 is a standard and effective play here.

I am holding 5d-2d, which is an extremely weak hand. Facing a raise from the button and a subsequent 3-bet from the small blind, my equity is significantly lower than the pot odds offered. Continuing in this hand with such a weak holding out of position would be negative expected value (-EV) and reckless. The disciplined play is to fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

4s5s is a great hand to open on the button due to its playability, but facing a large 3-bet from the Small Blind (to 850), its value drops significantly. We do have position, but the stack sizes and the size of the 3-bet don't give us the implied odds needed to profitably call with such a low speculative hand. Folding is the most standard and disciplined play here to preserve our stack.

I am on the button with Qh9c in a 3-handed game. This is a strong enough hand to open for a raise to put pressure on the blinds and potentially win the pot pre-flop. Q9o is well within the standard opening range for the button. Given the previous play, a raise to 250 (2.5x the big blind) is a standard sizing to take control of the hand with position.

I am in the small blind with 6s 4c, which is a very weak hand with poor equity. Calling 200 into a pot of 400 while being out of position against the button is a losing play. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

KTo is a reasonable holding in the Big Blind facing a Button open. We are getting good pot odds to defend, and the hand plays okay against a wide Button opening range. I'll flat call and evaluate on the flop.

We flopped top pair with a ten kicker on a board with a flush draw. Being out of position against the pre-flop raiser, the standard and most profitable play is to check to them. This allows Flash to continuation bet with bluffs or weaker hands, which we can then comfortably call, whereas leading out (donk betting) might fold out those weaker hands and isolate us against a stronger range.

I raised pre-flop with Queen-Nine offsuit from the button and was called by the Big Blind. The flop comes King-high (Ks 3s 6c). As the pre-flop aggressor, this board texture favors my range, as I am more likely to have strong Kings or Aces. The opponent checked to me, which is standard. I will place a continuation bet of approximately 45% of the pot to represent strength and attempt to take the pot down immediately. If the opponent calls or raises, I will likely give up on the hand unless a Queen or Nine improves my hand on the turn.

We flopped top pair with a decent kicker. Facing a standard continuation bet from the pre-flop raiser, calling is the optimal play. Raising here would likely fold out Flash's bluffs and worse hands, while isolating us against stronger Kings (like AK, KQ, KJ) or sets.
